EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain developed late on Christmas Eve and continued throughout the overnight before becoming heavier shortly before sunrise. Light ice accretion occurred, mainly on elevated surfaces during this time. Quite a few slick spots also developed, mainly on bridges and overpasses. Freezing rain continued through the morning hours along the eastern escarpment of the Blue Ridge, and became heavy at times by mid-morning. By the time the precipitation tapered off, ice accretion ranged from .25 to .5 inch across much of the area. The combination of ice and wind gusts up to 60 mph (with gusts up to 80 mph in the higher elevations) caused numerous trees and power lines to fall. The resultant widespread power outages exacerbated the problems for areas that had yet to recover from the December 18th snowstorm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were blown down in West Jefferson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Hurricane Ida soaked the region during November 12th. Winds gusting up to 35 mph and the wet ground made it easier for smaller trees to fall. One tree fell on a car, killing the driver in Rockingham county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell onto a car, resulting in one fatality. This occurred 3 miles south of Bethany, on Witty Road.

Strong wind gusts developed around sunrise across the southern mountains, blowing down numerous trees near the southern escarpment.

Freezing rain developed in some of the high valleys of the northern mountains during the evening. Ice accumulation was mainly confined to elevated surfaces. The freezing rain changed to rain soon after midnight.

The remnants from Hurricane Ida which made landfall along the Florida panhandle combined with a powerful upper level storm system along the Carolina coast. The result was a powerful low pressure system which developed near Cape Fear and tracked to Cape Hatteras. Between 3 to 6 inches of rain fell across central North Carolina over a 3 day period. Gusty winds to 35 to 45 mph combined with saturated soil resulted in power outages and some property damage from falling trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 40 to 45 mph along with heavy rain resulted in numerous trees falling and knocking down power lines.

Light freezing rain developed during the early morning hours across the foothills and southern mountains of North Carolina, producing light ice accretion on elevated surfaces. A few slick spots developed on bridges and overpasses before temperatures warmed above freezing later in the morning. The ice accretion did cause some isolated damage to trees and power lines.

Several hours of widespread moderate to heavy rain caused flooding and flash flooding across parts of western North Carolina, especially across the mountains. Rainfall amounts of 3 to 4 inches were common across the mountains, with 2 to 3 inch amounts over much of the western piedmont.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued along portions of Back Creek and the Rocky Broad River into the evening hours. Total rainfall amounts of 4-5 inches occurred, mostly within a 24-hour period.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to size of quarters fell near Edneyville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed over South Carolina during the early morning hours of Jan 20th 2009. The low pressure system tracked northeast and intensified offshore of the North Carolina coast during the rest of the day. Rain developed over eastern North Carolina after midnight on Jan 20th, and gradually changed to snow from west to east through the morning hours. Bands of snow dropped on average 3 to 6 inches across the northern counties with 1 to 3 inches south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow around 10 am and continue into the evening hours. Snow accumulated 2 to 4 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low level jet developed over eastern North Carolina ahead of a cold front. Bands of low topped convective showers helped mix these strong winds down to the ground producing numerous reports of damaging winds. One band of showers crossed during the late morning hours producing damage over mainly the northern parts of eastern North Carolina. Another band of showers crossed in the evening with damaging gusts over the entire region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roof partially blown off a warehouse. Large tree blown down onto home damaging roof. Numerous trees and powerlines blown down.

Bands of low topped convective showers helped mix these strong winds down to the ground producing numerous reports of damaging winds. One band of showers crossed during the late morning hours producing damage over mainly the northern parts of eastern North Carolina. Another band of showers crossed in the evening with damaging gusts over the entire region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees blown down on power lines with five thousand homes without electricity.

The remnants from Hurricane Ida which made landfall along the Florida panhandle combined with a powerful upper level storm system along the Carolina coast. The result was a powerful low pressure system which developed near Cape Fear and tracked to Cape Hatteras. Between 3 to 6 inches of rain fell across central North Carolina over a 3 day period. Gusty winds to 35 to 45 mph combined with saturated soil resulted in power outages and some property damage from falling trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 40 to 45 mph along with heavy rain resulted in numerous trees falling and knocking down power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong coastal low pressure sytem tracking from the Gulf of Mexico northeast along the Carolina coast collided with cold air resulting in a a major winter storm. The heavierst snow fell across the northwest piedmont, foothills and mountains of North Carolina where up to seven inches of snow in the Triad with over two feet of snow in portions the mountains. Numerous motorists were stranded on highways and interstates west and north of Wintson Salem. Hundreds of automobile accidents were reported in the northwest piedmont.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 1 to 3 inches of snow fell across Durham county. In the city of Durham an inch of snow accumulated on roads before snow changed over the rain. Further north over the northern portions of the county snow fell for a longer period of time resulting in around 3 inches of snow. Law enforecement responded to a number of accidents the evening of the snowfall.

Low pressure brought a period of moderate to heavy rain to the mountains of North Carolina Christmas eve and Christmas day. Around 3 inches of rain fell across much of the southern mountains of North Carolina which resulted in a few areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Official NWS stream gauges indicated several streams rose to levels well above established flood stages, including Mud Creek on the south side of Hendersonville, Bat Fork Creek on the east side and Clear Creek about 5 miles northeast of the city. Affected roads included Airport Rd, King St, Grove St, and Fruitland Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow during the early evening across portions of the foothills and the western Piedmont of North Carolina. Snow became heavy at times throughout the evening, and up to 4 inches had accumulated across the area by 10 pm. Snow, heavy at times and accompanied by occasional lightning, continued into the late evening and early overnight hours. By the time the snow tapered off, accumulations of 3-6 inches were common across the area. However, localized amounts of up to 9 inches were reported, especially along a corridor extending from Shelby to Hickory. The heavy wet snow caused quite a few trees and power lines to fall, resulting in numerous power outages. Some structures received minor to moderate roof damage due to the weight of the snow. Some customers were without power for several days. A tree fell on the library in Belmont, NC, causing damage to the roof. Numerous traffic accidents also occurred.

Rain changed to snow across the southwest foothills of North Carolina  during the late afternoon. The snow became heavy at times, particularly along the I-85 corridor. By mid-evening, 1-4 inches of accumulation was reported across much of the area. Snow, heavy at times, and accompanied by occasional lightning, continued into the evening hours. By the time the snow tapered off, accumulations of 3-6 inches were common across the area. However, localized amounts of up to 8 inches were reported along the I-85 corridor.  The heavy wet snow, combined with gusty winds, caused quite a few trees and power lines to fall, resulting in numerous power outages. Some customers were without power for several days. One tree fell on and damaged a home in Gaffney. In addition, there were numerous traffic accidents.

A slow moving cold front approached eastern North Carolina from the northwest during the afternoon of March 27th 2009. Weak instability developed ahead of the front and isolated thunderstorms developed during the early evening hours as an upper level disturbance crossed the region. Two tornadoes developed with the isolated thunderstorms over the inland sections of eastern North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Survey revealed an EF-1 tornado with winds estimated between 105 to 110 miles an hours touched down just west of Hookerton. The tornado produced minor to moderate damage to several homes and snapped several trees off. The tornado had a path length of nearly one mile and was about 100 yards wide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front approached eastern North Carolina from the northwest during the afternoon of March 27th 2009. Weak instability developed ahead of the front and isolated thunderstorms developed during the early evening hours as an upper level disturbance crossed the region. Two tornadoes developed with the isolated thunderstorms over the inland sections of eastern North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS survey revealed an EF-1 tornado with winds estimated between 85 and 90 mph touched down just southeast of Greenville. The tornado was on the ground a little over one half mile and was about one hundred yards wide. The tornado did minor damage to about forty homes off Ashcroft Drive resulting in about two hundred thousand dollars in damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strongly unstable air-mass with steep lapse rates below 600 mb was lifted by the sea breeze. Lift was also provided by the Piedmont/Sandhills trough which allowed convection to develop during the early to mid afternoon. By late afternoon and evening, numerous boundary collisions produced severe pulse storms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured hail up to the size of 1 and 1/8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow developed during the pre-dawn hours across the Piedmont. As snow continued to fall across the Piedmont, heavy snowfall amounts were reached across Cabarrus and Union Counties shortly before the snow completely tapered off. Accumulations ranged from 2-4 inches across the area, although a few higher amounts were reported in eastern portions of these counties.

Strong winds developed around noon along the I-85 corridor and persisted through the afternoon. Numerous trees were blown onto roads and power lines, resulting in quite a few power outages across the area. Some trees fell on automobiles, and one tree fell on a trailer in the Gaffney area, causing significant damage. Wet ground resulting from heavy rain during the preceding days likely contributed to the tree damage.

A prolonged period of rainfall resulted in flooding east of Charlotte.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quite a few roads were closed due to flood water across central and northern portions of the county. Some flooding was the result of overflowing streams, while some was caused by poor drainage. Affected roads included but were not limited to Macedonia Church Rd, Antioch Church Rd at Highview Rd, Howey Bottoms Rd at Duncan Rd, and Jackson and Benton Streets in the city of Monroe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light rain changed to light snow across the lower elevations of Caldwell and McDowell counties during the early afternoon, and continued off an on through the evening. Although up to 4 inches of accumulation were reported across extreme eastern portions of the area, with even higher totals in the higher elevations across the extreme west, a shadowing effect from the mountains created a relative minimum in snowfall across much of the lower elevations, as much of the area reported amounts ranging from a dusting to 2 inches.

Scattered thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and early evening hours as an upper level impulse moved across. Some of the storms became severe producing hail, gusty winds and isolated tornadoes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level system moved across Central North Carolina in a northwest flow. A surface low pressure center developed near the Triad the evening of February 3rd and then tracked southeast and east across Central North Carolina. This resulted in a band of two to four inches of snowfall no wider than 20 miles. Given the warm ground temperatures and short duration of the snow roads were clear shortly after daybreak on February 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between one to three inches of snow fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low level jet developed over eastern North Carolina ahead of a cold front. Bands of low topped convective showers helped mix these strong winds down to the ground producing numerous reports of damaging winds. One band of showers crossed during the late morning hours producing damage over mainly the northern parts of eastern North Carolina. Another band of showers crossed in the evening with damaging gusts over the entire region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter measured fifteen second wind gust to 78 mph near Washington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed over South Carolina during the early morning hours of Jan 20th 2009. The low pressure system tracked northeast and intensified offshore of the North Carolina coast during the rest of the day. Rain developed over eastern North Carolina after midnight on Jan 20th, and gradually changed to snow from west to east through the morning hours. Bands of snow dropped on average 3 to 6 inches across the northern counties with 1 to 3 inches south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow around 7 am and continue into the early evening hours. Snow accumulated 3 to 4 inches across the county.

Many of the thunderstorms that developed became severe and produced damaging winds across the eastern half of central North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was reported down on a house near 4th Street and East Hamlet Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated cell developed and tracked along a front in the vicinity of a surface wave near the North Carolina and South Carolina State line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst began just west of Scotch Meadows Country Club near Fairway Drive and continued east for almost 2 miles before dissipating at Hasty Road. The microburt knocked down numerous trees which resulted in minor structural damage to 3 homes east of the Country Club. As the microburst progressed east it also destroyed 2 barns at a horse farm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an extended period of snow showers, heavy snowfall accumulations were reached across the northern mountains by mid-afternoon on the 7th. Total accumulations were generally in the 2 to 4 inch range across the area, but amounts approaching 10 inches occurred in the higher elevations along the Tennessee border.

After an extended period of snow showers, heavy snowfall accumulations were reached across the northern mountains by mid-afternoon on the 7th. After an extended period of snow showers, heavy snowfall accumulations were reached across the higher elevations of the southern and central mountains near the Tennessee border. Total accumulations in areas above 3500 feet generally ranged from 3-6 inches, although some of the higher elevations of the Smokies, the Newfound Mountains, and along the Cherohala Skyway received amounts of 10 inches or more.

Training pulse severe thunderstorms produced large hail, wind damage and severe flash flooding over downtown Greensboro as 4 to 6 inches of rain fell over the area. There were numerous road closures water rescues with one flash flood related fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous streets were closed in downtown Greensboro with as many as 50 to 100 water rescues performed, mainly from stalled out vehicles. Multiple buildings were flooded, with at least 10 to 15 on the UNC-Greenbsoro Campus alone. In addition, as many as two dozen businesses and government buildings were also flooded. One fatality occurred when a woman lost control of a moped and went into a creek. A police officer rescued the woman, only to have her jump back into the creek in an attempt to recover her moped.

The unseasonably dry low levels of the atmosphere across central North Carolina created a favorable environment for any thunderstorms that developed to produce damaging winds. Many of the thunderstorms that developed became severe and produced damaging winds across the eastern half of central North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported in the Bedford Subdivison, near the intersection of Cardington Lane and Rogerson Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 20, 2009 the first major winter storm to strike Central North Carolina since 2004 brought 3 to 7 inches of snow to most of the area. A strong upper level disturbace dropped quickly southeast into the mid-Atlantic follwed by a surface of low pressure which developed south of Wilmington. The combination of cold air and moisture surging in from the coast allowed the rain to transition to snow between midnight and 3 am. Snow lasted until afternoon in many locations and did not taper off in the Interstate 95 corridor until 3 pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 4 to 5 inches of snow fell across the county over a 12 hour period. Roads were quickly covered with snow resulting in several traffic accidents and the closing of local schools and businesses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very powerful upper level trough in combination with high wind in the low and mid levels of the atmosphere generated a prolonged period of strong damaging winds from 9 am in the morning through the afternoon and into the evening. Widespread power outages along with areas of damage were reported across much of Central North Carolina. A band of convective showers which developed in the morning also generated damaging winds over portions of the eastern piedmont and coastal plain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds between 55 to 65 mph created widespread wind damage across Wake county. Numerous trees were blown down countywide. Some of the worst damage occurred in Cary when 2 large oak trees fell onto 2 cars and a home. Over 4000 people were without power.

A nearly stationary line of thunderstorms developed just inland of the coast along the sea breeze during the afternoon of August 12, 2009. Torrential rain occurred for several hours centered near Havelock. Rainfall amounts of 6 to 10 inches were reported in a three to four hour period leading to significant flooding in the Havelock area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain produced flooding at the intersection of Western Boulevard and Brynn Marr Road. Several cars stalled in the flood waters.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant ocean overwash occurring during high tide. Highway 12 is flooded and closed.

A band of convective showers which developed in the morning also generated damaging winds over portions of the eastern piedmont and coastal plain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were blown down across White Oak Road near Marion.

On January 20, 2009 the first major winter storm to strike Central North Carolina since 2004 brought 3 to 7 inches of snow to most of the area. A strong upper level disturbace dropped quickly southeast into the mid-Atlantic follwed by a surface of low pressure which developed south of Wilmington. The combination of cold air and moisture surging in from the coast allowed the rain to transition to snow between midnight and 3 am. Snow lasted until afternoon in many locations and did not taper off in the Interstate 95 corridor until 3 pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 3 to 4 inches of snow fell across the county over a 12 hour period. Roads were quickly covered with snow resulting in several traffic accidents and the closing of local schools and businesses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical air mass was in place, with a weak front north across Virginia. Southeast flow into the mountains and the frontal boundary set the stage for heavy rain producing showers and thunderstorms. Heavy rains across southern Wilkes County dumped close to 4 inches in a short amount of time. Flash flooding occurred across the Moravian, Hunting and Warrior creek basins...inundating roads and homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Warrior Creek flooded State Route 18, 2 miles southwest of Boomer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Ida interacted with a strong upper level disturbance to produce a deep, slow moving, low pressure system off the Carolina coast November 11th through the 13th. This system produced widespread heavy rain, gusty winds, and coastal flooding across portions of eastern North Carolina. By far, the most signifcant impact was coastal flooding over the northern Outer Banks where over five million dollars in damage was reported. The large waves from the storm continued to batter the Outer Banks for several days after the storm system moved away.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eastern sections of Carteret County were flooded from the Pamlico Sound. Water levels two to three feet above normal from Cedar Island to Beaufort resulted in flooding of many low lying secondary roads.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding near Highway 24 in Cedar Point. This resulted in water entering a residence near Hill Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low level jet developed over eastern North Carolina ahead of a cold front. Bands of low topped convective showers helped mix these strong winds down to the ground producing numerous reports of damaging winds. One band of showers crossed during the late morning hours producing damage over mainly the northern parts of eastern North Carolina. Another band of showers crossed in the evening with damaging gusts over the entire region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust of 60 mph measured by National Weather Service instrument at the Alligator River Bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of saturated ground resulting from 3 to 5 inches of rain and gusty winds from the remnants of tropical storm Ida, and a developing cyclone off the Carolina coast, resulted in quite a few uprooted trees across the piedmont and foothills. Some trees fell on roads and power lines, resulting in periodic road closures and scattered power outages. Several homes and vehicles were damaged by falling trees across the area. Damage was most concentrated in the North Carolina piedmont.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very windy conditions downed several trees and power lines. A few trees fell on homes, with at least one home in the Newton area sustaining damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Ida interacted with a strong upper level disturbance to produce a deep, slow moving, low pressure system off the Carolina coast November 11th through the 13th. This system produced widespread heavy rain, gusty winds, and coastal flooding across portions of eastern North Carolina. By far, the most signifcant impact was coastal flooding over the northern Outer Banks where over five million dollars in damage was reported. The large waves from the storm continued to batter the Outer Banks for several days after the storm system moved away.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant ocean over-wash and coastal flooding developed over the northern Outer Banks during the early morning hours of November 12th and continued through the early moring hours of November 14th. Areas from Buxton north to Duck had several episodes of coastal flooding, mainly during the high tide cycle. Overall 4 homes were destroyed, 61 had major damage and 465 had minor damage. Highway 12 was severely flooded and destoyed near Rodanthe due to the ocean over-wash. Damage from the coastal flooding was estimated to be 5.8 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex developed along a boundary and tracked across southwest North Carolina bringing a few thunderstorm wind damage reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported several trees downed by thunderstorm winds in Murphy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept offshore during the morning of November 27th. Gusty west to northwest winds in the wake of the front produced minor sound-side flooding over portions of the Outer Banks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two feet of water was reported on Highway 12 near Avon due to sound-side flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept offshore during the morning of November 27th. Gusty west to northwest winds in the wake of the front produced minor sound-side flooding over portions of the Outer Banks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of Ocracoke Village was flooded from the Pamlico Sound with water levels 2 to 3 feet higher than normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very powerful upper level trough in combination with high wind in the low and mid levels of the atmosphere generated a prolonged period of strong damaging winds from 9 am in the morning through the afternoon and into the evening. Widespread power outages along with areas of damage were reported across much of Central North Carolina. A band of convective showers which developed in the morning also generated damaging winds over portions of the eastern piedmont and coastal plain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds between 55 to 65 mph created widespread wind damage across the county. Numerous trees were blown down countywide. Four people were injured when a tree fell onto a mobile home on Leisure Drive in Pinetops.|Winds also ripped the roof of another mobile home at Baker Mobile Home Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very powerful upper level trough in combination with high wind in the low and mid levels of the atmosphere generated a prolonged period of strong damaging winds from 9 am in the morning through the afternoon and into the evening. Widespread power outages along with areas of damage were reported across much of Central North Carolina. A band of convective showers which developed in the morning also generated damaging winds over portions of the eastern piedmont and coastal plain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds between 55 to 65 mph caused widespread tree damage and power outages across the county during the late afternoon and evening. The high winds completely destroyed an out building on Stamper Siding Road, near Scotland neck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 20, 2009 the first major winter storm to strike Central North Carolina since 2004 brought 3 to 7 inches of snow to most of the area. A strong upper level disturbace dropped quickly southeast into the mid-Atlantic follwed by a surface of low pressure which developed south of Wilmington. The combination of cold air and moisture surging in from the coast allowed the rain to transition to snow between midnight and 3 am. Snow lasted until afternoon in many locations and did not taper off in the Interstate 95 corridor until 3 pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 4 to 5 inches of snow fell across the county over a 12 hour period. Roads were quickly covered with snow resulting in several traffic accidents and the closing of local schools and businesses. One fatality was reported when a driver skidded on a road and hit a tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy rain fell between the 18th and 22nd across the southern mountains of North Carolina. Over the five day period, over 17 inches of rain fall in a few locations, with many areas reporting 10 inches of more. The most significant flooding started early  in the morning on the 20th when three nearly stationary areas thunderstorms produced very heavy rain along the eastern escarpment of the Blue Ridge. Widespread flooding of streams and some large rivers developed over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding developed along the Green River, forcing evacuations and closing Green River Cove Rd. Water reportedly entered several homes along the River, with water up to the windows of one home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Ida interacted with a strong upper level disturbance to produce a deep, slow moving, low pressure system off the Carolina coast November 11th through the 13th. This system produced widespread heavy rain, gusty winds, and coastal flooding across portions of eastern North Carolina. By far, the most signifcant impact was coastal flooding over the northern Outer Banks where over five million dollars in damage was reported. The large waves from the storm continued to batter the Outer Banks for several days after the storm system moved away.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large waves swept a woman off some sand bags near Rodanthe and carried her out into the ocean where she apparently drowned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On New Year's Eve a period of light freezing rain resulted in light ice accumulations on elevated surface from the Triad east across the Virginia border counties north of Raleigh.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain was reported across the area resulting in a light coating of ice on elevated surfaces such as trees, bushes and power lines. Area roads remained clear.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 20, 2009 the first major winter storm to strike Central North Carolina since 2004 brought 3 to 7 inches of snow to most of the area. A strong upper level disturbace dropped quickly southeast into the mid-Atlantic follwed by a surface of low pressure which developed south of Wilmington. The combination of cold air and moisture surging in from the coast allowed the rain to transition to snow between midnight and 3 am. Snow lasted until afternoon in many locations and did not taper off in the Interstate 95 corridor until 3 pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 4 to 6 inches of snow fell across the county over a 12 hour period. Roads were quickly covered with snow resulting in several traffic accidents and the closing of local schools and businesses. One indirect fatality was reported the following morning when a vehicle hit a patch of ice on NC Highway 97 near Leggett and then collided with an oncoming semitruck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several hours of widespread moderate to heavy rain caused flooding and flash flooding across parts of western North Carolina, especially across the mountains. Rainfall amounts of 3 to 4 inches were common across the mountains, with 2 to 3 inch amounts over much of the western piedmont.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Old River Road was closed due to flooding along the Tuckaseegee River about a mile east of Bryson City. Also, Needmore Rd was closed due to flooding of the Little Tennessee River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several hours of widespread moderate to heavy rain caused flooding and flash flooding across parts of western North Carolina, especially across the mountains. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although heavy rain began to taper off during the pre-dawn hours, flooding persisted into the early afternoon in response to runoff from 3 to 4 inches of storm total rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Ida pushed north from the Gulf Coast, and brought a prolonged period of moderate to heavy rain November 10th-12th. Widespread stream and river flooding occurred, with rainfall of 3 to 7 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to seven inches of rain fell across the county November 10th through the 12th. This caused several streams and creeks to flood, as well as, the Mayo and Dan River. The flooding closed many roads throughout the northern half of Rockingham County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed over South Carolina during the early morning hours of Jan 20th 2009. The low pressure system tracked northeast and intensified offshore of the North Carolina coast during the rest of the day. Rain developed over eastern North Carolina after midnight on Jan 20th, and gradually changed to snow from west to east through the morning hours. Bands of snow dropped on average 3 to 6 inches across the northern counties with 1 to 3 inches south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow around 7 am and continued into the early evening hours. Snow accumulated 3 to 5 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak low pressure just inland from the coast produced widespread showers and isolated thunderstorms near the southern coast of eastern North Carolina during the early morning hours of November 19th. Locally heavy rain produced minor flooding over portions of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: West Fire Tower Road north of Cape Carteret was flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept across the mountains of North Carolina during the morning of November 27th. Northwest winds increased substantially, gusting to 60 mph and downing a couple of trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds were at or above 40 mph at times during this period, with gusts to 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong coastal low pressure sytem tracking from the Gulf of Mexico northeast along the Carolina coast collided with cold air resulting in a a major winter storm. The heavierst snow fell across the northwest piedmont, foothills and mountains of North Carolina where up to seven inches of snow in the Triad with over two feet of snow in portions the mountains. Numerous motorists were stranded on highways and interstates west and north of Wintson Salem. Hundreds of automobile accidents were reported in the northwest piedmont.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 3 to 5 inches of snow fell across Vance county accumulating on all major highways making travel impossible.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low topped tornadic supercells developed along a stationary front located across the coastal plain region of North Carolina. The storms intensified as a shortwave trough moved northeast across the area, producing EF-1 damage in Cumberland County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down and snapped in a wooded area behind an open field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front and powerful upper level trough swept across central North Carolina late in the afternoon into the early evening hours on September 28, 2009. A band of showers and thunderstorms formed as the cold front moved east of the mountains and into central North Carolina. Some of these storms became severe and produced damaging winds and hail to the size of nickels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power-lines were reported down at the intersection of Doggett and Beckett Roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure combined with deep moisture caused flooding in Pender County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of heavy snow moved across Central North Carolina blanketing the region with 2 to 8 inches of snow. Areas in the piedmont experienced the most snow fall with the Triad reporting up to 8 inches. The heavy snow was the result of a surface low pressure system tracking east out of the Ohio River Valley combining with cold air from the north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between one to two inches of snow fell countywide resulting in slippery roads.

On January 20, 2009 the first major winter storm to strike Central North Carolina since 2004 brought 3 to 7 inches of snow to most of the area. A strong upper level disturbace dropped quickly southeast into the mid-Atlantic follwed by a surface of low pressure which developed south of Wilmington. The combination of cold air and moisture surging in from the coast allowed the rain to transition to snow between midnight and 3 am. Snow lasted until afternoon in many locations and did not taper off in the Interstate 95 corridor until 3 pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 4 to 6 inches of snow fell across the county over a 12 hour period. Roads were quickly covered with snow resulting in several traffic accidents and the closing of local schools and businesses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front and powerful upper level trough swept across central North Carolina late in the afternoon into the early evening hours on September 28, 2009. A band of showers and thunderstorms formed as the cold front moved east of the mountains and into central North Carolina. Some of these storms became severe and produced damaging winds and hail to the size of nickels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was reported down on Chestnut Ridge Church Road at Holmes Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boy drowned after getting caught in a rip current at Fort Fisher.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A boy went under the water at Fort Fisher State Park. Two adults and lifeguards attempted to rescue him, but the boy was found dead a couple of hours later in shallow water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Nearly stationary storms produced heavy rain in St. Pauls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused standing water on several streets in St. Pauls.  About a foot of water was reported on Main Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Localized heavy rain was reported in northeast Brunswick County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads in the community of Brunswick Forest were flooded. One road had up to two feet of standing water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure offshore of the coast and high pressure to the north resulted in a prolonged period of north to northeast winds along the coast. These winds produced rough surf and ocean over-wash was reported along Highway 12 near Rodanthe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High surf conditions produced ocean over-wash at Mirlo Beach near Rodanthe. The over-wash closed Highway 12 for a period around 7 am during high tide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a deep moist air mass in place across North Carolina, a mid-level shortwave trough moved across the area during the afternoon and evening. The Falcon community received around one inch or rainfall during the late morning hours with a long lull during the early afternoon before training thunderstorms dumped an additional 5 inches between 630 to 900 pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three feet of flood waters was flowing over Northwest and Brooks Streets in town. NC Highway 82 was closed due to the flood waters. Flood waters entered 3 structures.  Only one structure received damage, a day care center which flooded several inches deep on the ground floor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first high elevation snowfall of the season occurred as moist northwest flow developed behind a strong cold front, resulting in scattered snow showers. Accumulations were mainly confined to areas along the Tennessee border, above 4000 feet, where as much as 2 inches fell. However, locations as far south as the high elevations above Lake Toxaway saw a dusting.
Upslope winds helped support a few strong storms over the North Carolina foothills. One of these storms produced a small area of large hail and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported penny size hail and measured a wind gust of 56 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning ignited a fire at a home on Robinwood Rd on the southeast side of Gastonia. The fire caused significant damage to the attic and a bedroom.
Lightning started a couple house fires over western North Carolina during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning ignited a fire at a home on Wilson Rd, about two miles east northeast of Brevard, causing damage to the roof and attic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moved northeast just inland from the coast during the early morning hours of December 19th. This low produced strong winds along the coast, with localized coastal flooding along the Outer Banks.

Strong low pressure moved northeast just inland from the coast during the early morning hours of December 19th. This low produced strong winds along the coast, with localized coastal flooding along the Outer Banks.
The remnants of the tropical system Ida moved up the eastern seaboard, bringing heavy rain and wind to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds and saturated soil combined to blow trees down on Hwy 211, 71 and 41.

Accumulations were mainly confined to areas along the Tennessee border, above 4000 feet, where as much as 2 inches fell. However, locations as far south as the high elevations above Lake Toxaway saw a dusting.

Weak low level meso-scale convective vorticity maximum over middle Tennessee created forcing for ascent. Pulse convection developed in a region of moderate instability and weak deep layer shear affecting Southeast Tennessee and Southwest North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were reported down from Pine Log to Hayesville.

A large rock slide occurred near mile marker 3 on Interstate 40. The slide was thought to be the result of recent wet weather and continuous freezing/thawing of groundwater. The slide resulted in I-40 being closed for several months.

A strong low pressure tracked across southwest North Carolina bringing damaging non-thunderstorm winds to the area in the overnight hours on the 25th. The strongest winds occurred over the higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 3 trees and a powerline downed by non-thunderstorm winds in Warne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening line of thunderstorms moved out of Tennessee and into the North Carolina Mountains. One of the storms produced an isolated area of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two small trees were snapped off on Revere Rd in the northeast part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm produced some wind damage over the southwest mountains of North Carolina during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three trees were blown down on highway 64 near county road 1442 about 4 miles southwest of Franklin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken squall line with several discrete mini-supercells out in advance of the line moved through the area during the evening. Heavy rain and strong wind gusts accompanied the convection. Severe thunderstorms did produce isolated damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree fell onto a power line near the intersection of Maco Road and Hunters Ridge Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken squall line with several discrete mini-supercells out in advance of the line moved through the area during the evening. Heavy rain and strong wind gusts accompanied the convection. Severe thunderstorms did produce isolated damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter estimated a wind gust to 60 to 70 mph near the intersection of Stone House Road and State Road 1402.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree fell onto a power line, taking the power line down as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain developed during the evening hours across the mountains and portions of the foothills and piedmont of North Carolina. Ice accretion was mainly limited to elevated surfaces.

A strengthening area of low pressure moved out of the Gulf of Mexico, across southern Georgia, and then up the southeast coast. As the low passed south of the region, snow became heavy across the southern and central mountains, as well as the Smokies and surrounding valleys late in the morning. Heavy snow developed a little later over the northern mountains. The heavy snow continued throughout the afternoon. Snowfall rates of 1-2 inches per hour became common across the area during the afternoon. Meanwhile, warming temperatures allowed the snow to mix with and eventually change to rain and sleet in the southwest mountain valleys. The heavy, wet snow combined with gusty winds to cause numerous trees and power lines to fall across the area during the afternoon. Widespread power outages resulted, and some customers were without power for as much as a week. Even longer outages affected parts of the northern mountains. ||The snow ended over the Blue Ridge and the central mountains on the evening of the 18th. However, wrap around snow showers developed along the Tennessee line, resulting in additional snow accumulations overnight and into the morning hours of the 19th.||Total accumulations ranged from 12-18 inches across the lower northern mountain valleys, to 2-3 feet in the higher elevations along the Tennessee border, and in areas along the eastern escarpment. Over the southern and central mountains, total accumulations ranged from 6-10 inches in the lower elevations near the southern escarpment, to as much as 2 feet in the higher elevations. While the southwest mountain valleys generally saw only 3-5 inches, 2-3 feet of total snowfall was reported in the higher elevations of the Smokies and along the Cherohala Skyway in Graham County.||Hundreds of traffic accidents were reported during the storm, and continued for several days thereafter, as continuous melting and refreezing of ice and snow resulted in treacherous road conditions during the late night and morning hours. Hospitals reported 100s of cases and slips and falls during this time as well.

A mix of rain, sleet, and snow developed across the I-85 corridor throughout the morning. Up to 3 inches of snow fell before precipitation changed over to sleet and then rain. Although the precipitation changed to mainly rain during the afternoon, temperatures that hovered right around freezing allowed for some light ice accretion to occur on elevated surfaces.

An intense Nor'easter produced moderate to severe coastal flooding across much of the Currituck Outer Banks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several streets, homes and businesses were flooded in low lying areas of the county close or directly exposed to the Currituck Sound. Moyock experienced the heaviest flooding due to rising Sound waters. Moyock area had 3 neighborhoods that flooded, and flood water going into some of the homes.

A strengthening area of low pressure moved out of the Gulf of Mexico, across southern Georgia, and then up the southeast coast. As the low passed south of the region, snow became heavy across the southern and central mountains, as well as the Smokies and surrounding valleys late in the morning. Heavy snow developed a little later over the northern mountains. The heavy snow continued throughout the afternoon. Snowfall rates of 1-2 inches per hour became common across the area during the afternoon. Meanwhile, warming temperatures allowed the snow to mix with and eventually change to rain and sleet in the southwest mountain valleys. The heavy, wet snow combined with gusty winds to cause numerous trees and power lines to fall across the area during the afternoon. Widespread power outages resulted, and some customers were without power for as much as a week. Even longer outages affected parts of the northern mountains. ||The snow ended over the Blue Ridge and the central mountains on the evening of the 18th. A strong low pressure tracked across southwest North Carolina bringing damaging non-thunderstorm winds to the area during the morning hours on the 9th.  The strongest winds occurred over the higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Utility company personnel reported several trees downed by non-thunderstorm winds countywide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong coastal low pressure area produced moderate coastal flooding across much of the Currituck Outer Banks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak tide height at Duck was 6.27 feet above MLLW.  Several streets, homes and businesses were flooded in low lying areas of the county close or directly exposed to the Atlantic Ocean.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong coastal low pressure area produced moderate coastal flooding across much of the Currituck Outer Banks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several streets, homes and businesses were flooded in low lying areas of the county close or directly exposed to the Currituck Sound.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure raced from the High Plains early on December 8th to the upper Great Lakes by early on the 9th while deepening rapidly and bringing a complex series of fronts across the area. Strong west winds of 20 to 40 mph occurred on the afternoon of the 9th with gusts over 60 mph estimated along some ridges and mountain peaks. Boone AWOS had a measured wind gust of 56 mph from the west around 320 PM on December 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was reported blown down near Laurel Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure tracked from Texas through Iowa from December 24-25 bringing precipitation which began as freezing rain in many areas due to trapped cold air at the surface. The cold air persisted long enough to allow significant icing of 1/4 up to 1 inch in some locations, despite very strong east to southeasterly flow of milder air just off the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One-quarter inch of ice was on trees with some limbs brought down.

An intense Nor'easter produced moderate to severe coastal flooding across much of the Currituck Outer Banks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak tide height at Duck was 7.20 feet above MLLW, which was 3.22 feet above the astronomical tide. Numerous streets, homes and businesses were flooded in low lying areas of the county close or directly exposed to the Atlantic Ocean, especially in the Corolla and Carova Beach areas. There was also severe beach erosion and loss of protective dunes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very powerful upper level trough in combination with high wind in the low and mid levels of the atmosphere generated a prolonged period of strong damaging winds from 9 am in the morning through the afternoon and into the evening. Widespread power outages along with areas of damage were reported across much of Central North Carolina. A band of convective showers which developed in the morning also generated damaging winds over portions of the eastern piedmont and coastal plain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds between 55 to 65 mph created widespread wind damage across the county. Numerous trees were blown down countywide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening area of low pressure moved out of the Gulf of Mexico, across southern Georgia, and then up the southeast coast. As the low passed south of the region, snow became heavy across the foothills and piedmont during the afternoon, and continued to fall heavily throughout the afternoon and evening. Snowfall rates of 1-2 inches per hour became common over the foothills. The heavy, wet snow combined with gusty winds to cause a few trees and power lines to fall. Scattered power outages were reported. ||Total accumulations over the foothills ranged from 4-6 inches in the lower elevations near the piedmont to as much as 14 inches closer to the Blue Ridge. Over the piedmont, the snow mixed with rain and sleet at times, which cut down on the amount of accumulation, especially in areas closer to the I-85 corridor. Total accumulations ranged from 2 inches near the I-85 corridor, to 6 or 7 inches in areas along and north of I-40.||After the storm ended,  continuous melting and refreezing of ice and snow resulted in several mornings of treacherous driving across the area, with numerous accidents reported.

Strong low pressure moved northeast just west of the region during the late evening of December 2nd. As the low lifted to the north, a strong cold front approached eastern North Carolina. The air-mass ahead of the cold front was very warm and moist. Scattered thunderstorms developed with one becoming severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds near La Grange blew skirting off a mobile home. An aluminum car port was also blown down.

A strong upper level system moved across Central North Carolina in a northwest flow. A surface low pressure center developed near the Triad the evening of February 3rd and then tracked southeast and east across Central North Carolina. This resulted in a band of two to four inches of snowfall no wider than 20 miles. Given the warm ground temperatures and short duration of the snow roads were clear shortly after daybreak on February 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between two to three inches of snow fell countywide. Some locations reported up to four inches. Due to the warm ground temperatures snow melted rapidly of roads.

Low pressure tracked from the northeast Gulf coast early on December 18th reaching a position near Alma, Georgia as a 997 mb low by Friday the 18th at 5 PM EST. The storm continued moving northeast and deepened to a 986 mb low near Cape Hatteras by 10 AM December 19th. Heavy snow began around midday on the 18th and snows rapidly accumulated to warning criteria levels by late afternoon or early evening in all of the North Carolina counties. All forms of travel were rendered extremely difficult for several days due to this storm and numerous vehicle accidents were reported. Final snow totals ranged from about 5 inches in Caswell County to 17 inches in parts of Ashe County. This was the biggest snowstorm to affect northwest North Carolina since the January 6-8th, 1996 storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall ranged from 5 to 6 inches across the county, with the highest totals in the northwest. Travel remained very difficult throughout the storm.

A stationary boundary was located from GSB to POB southward through the Piedmont of South Carolina at mid-afternoon. A short-wave across the Georgia-South Carolina border was lifting northward across the area during the afternoon. Instability was marginal. Winds were virtually unidirectional, but there was modest speed shear through the lowest 3 kft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey confirmed an EF-2 Tornado touched down about 3 miles east of Parkton, North Carolina. The tornado first touched down along W Parkton Tobermory Road, halfway between Highway 301 and Interstate 95, with no significant damage. The tornado tracked north-northeast and intensified to EF-2 with winds to 130 mph as it impacted structures along E Everette Road. One mobile home was completely destroyed and an adult female was thrown by the tornado. The woman suffered minor injuries. Also at this location, the tornado destroyed a two story home that was empty at the time. The tornado continued to track to the north-northeast and eventually lifted across a wooded area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled surface front and back building convection produced heavy rains in Wilmington, NC.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall produced multiple reports of flooded cars and flooded streets.  There were several stalled cars on Racine Drive with standing water up to the hoods of the vehicles, and cars were stranded at the 1000 block of Rankin Drive and Wagner Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled surface front and back building convection produced heavy rains in Wilmington, NC.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New Hanover County  911 reports two instances of a vehicle  pushed by flood waters into a retention pond.  One occurred in the Sam's Club parking lot on College Road and the other in the Frog Pond Place apartment complex on Darlington Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled surface front and back building convection produced heavy rains in Wilmington, NC.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At three apartment complexes on Racine Drive the first floor apartments were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary was located from GSB to POB southward through the Piedmont of South Carolina at mid-afternoon. A short-wave across the Georgia-South Carolina border was lifting northward across the area during the afternoon. Instability was marginal. Winds were virtually unidirectional, but there was modest speed shear through the lowest 3 kft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey confirmed an EF-0 Tornado with winds up to 65 mph touched down about 6 miles southeast of Lumberton, North Carolina. The tornado skipped along a track to the north-northeast and caused sporadic damage along its path. Eyewitnesses indicated that two funnel clouds merged at the time of the touchdown near Pope Crossing Road. At this location a shed was destroyed and a mobile home suffered minor damage. In addition, there were several large limbs down in the area. There was another brief touchdown just south of the intersection of Beulah Church Road and Old Whiteville Road. At this location two sheds were destroyed and approximately twelve trees were knocked down. Witnesses indicated that the tornado lifted prior to crossing Old Whiteville Road and moved into a swampy area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds combined with an upper disturbance moving across the southern Appalachians brought heavy snowfall to the higher elevations of northwest North Carolina. This occurred from the evening of February 3rd through the early morning of February 5th. Snowfall amounts above 3000 feet close to the Tennessee border ranged from four to six inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to six inches of snow fell across the higher elevations of Western Ashe County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in a small part of the North Carolina Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streams flooded bridges and secondary roads across the western part of the county, after 5 to 6 inches of rain fell over a period of several hours. Many roads were closed. There were also quite a few minor mudslides and trees uprooted due to saturated ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked east across North and South Carolina, with enough cold air in place to produce a couple inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow showers tracked east across the region, with several spotters reporting nearly two inches of snow on grassy surfaces. Most of the main roads were not affected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed southeast of a quasi-stationary front located from the southern Appalachians, northeastward into Central NC. The storms produced penny size hail in the Sandhills.
Severe thunderstorms developed southeast of a quasi-stationary front located from the southern Appalachians, northeastward into Central NC. The storms produced penny size hail in the Sandhills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail lasted for one minute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low topped tornadic supercells developed along a stationary front located across the coastal plain region of North Carolina. The storms intensified as a shortwave trough moved northeast across the area, producing EF-1 damage in Cumberland County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado touched down just north of the Robeson County line and southeast of Hope Mills in the Roslin community. The tornado damage track was about 50 yards wide and stretched northeast for approximately 5 miles. On Roslin Farm Road, a brick home lost more than eighty percent of its roof structure. Several sheds and outbuildings in the area were also destroyed. A neighboring house also suffered minor structural damage to the garage. The tornado then tracked northeast over bare fields, before reaching Braxton Road. ||At Braxton Road the tornado continued with EF-1 intensity winds causing significant damage to a recently built two-story home.  The home was condemned when it experienced around twenty percent roof loss with major damage to the upstairs structure. Numerous sheds and fences were also blown over in the area. ||The tornado continued northeast crossing Chicken Foot Road and Corporation Drive, along which several trees were snapped. The tornado crossed Interstate 95 where numerous trees were blown down and snapped near Tim Starling Road. It was just north of this intersection where a tractor trailer was overturned by the high winds. Just west of Interstate 95 near the intersection of Tim Starling Road and Research Drive, a large industrial building suffered significant roof damage. The wind of the tornado caused the roof to buckle enough for the Fire Marshall to order an evacuation of the building. Rocks on the roof of the building were blown into the parking lot shattering the windows of numerous vehicles. A house currently under construction on Tim Starling Drive also sustained significant damage.||The tornado continued tracking northeast along Research Road while weakening to an EF-0 as it approached Claude Lee Road. Numerous trees were blown down and sheds were destroyed along Catherine Drive and Arlie Drive. Several homes in the area lost shingles and suffered minor damage to trim and siding. The tornado then lifted off the ground just as it approached the Fayetteville Regional Airport.

Snow showers developed over the western mountains during the late evening of the 18th, and continued off and on through the evening hours of the 20th. Snowfall totals ranged from 4 to 6 inches at the higher elevations of the southwest and central mountains, to 1 to 3 inches in the valleys. Over the northern mountains totals ranged from 4 to 7 inches, with 1 to 3 inches along the eastern slopes of the Blue Ridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through northwest North Carolina during the early evening of February 11th. A tight gradient existed between the low pressure over the northeast, and high pressure in the lower Mississippi Valley. This brought a period of high winds to the mountains and foothills through the morning of February 12th, which brought down some trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted as high as 70 mph across portions of Ashe County during the evening of February 11th, through early afternoon February 12th. The measured gust of 70 mph occurred at the West Jefferson Airport. North Carolina Department of Highways reported trees were blown down near Othello and along Golf Course Road, near West Jefferson.

Northwest winds combined with an upper disturbance moving across the southern Appalachians brought heavy snowfall to the higher elevations of northwest North Carolina. This occurred from the evening of February 3rd through the early morning of February 5th. Snowfall amounts above 3000 feet close to the Tennessee border ranged from four to six inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to six inches of snow fell across the higher elevations of Watauga County during the late afternoon of February 3rd, until the early morning of February 5th. The highest amounts were in the Beech Mountain area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe hail storms affected the western Piedmont of North Carolina during the afternoon hours.
A few severe hail storms affected the western Piedmont of North Carolina during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was reported on Jennings Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow across the northern mountains during the early afternoon hours. The snow became heavy at times and quickly accumulated to 1-4 inches by late afternoon. Snow, heavy at times, continued into the evening hours across. By the time the snow tapered off, accumulations of 4-8 inches were common across the area. However, some of the higher peaks received over a foot of snow. The heavy wet snow, combined with gusty winds, caused some trees to fall and isolated power outages.
A cold front tracked across southwest North Carolina during the afternoon and evening hours.  The resulting squall line triggered scattered thunderstorm wind damage reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement personnel reported several trees downed by thunderstorm winds in the Fire Creek area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow across the northern mountains during the early afternoon hours. The snow became heavy at times and quickly accumulated to 1-4 inches by late afternoon. Snow, heavy at times, continued into the evening hours across. By the time the snow tapered off, accumulations of 4-8 inches were common across the area. However, some of the higher peaks received over a foot of snow. The heavy wet snow, combined with gusty winds, caused some trees to fall and isolated power outages.

Rain changed to snow across portions of the southern and central mountains, generally in locations from the Balsams to areas north and east, and continued through the afternoon. The snow became heavy at times, and quickly accumulated to 1-4 inches by early evening. Locally higher amounts were reported in the higher elevations of the Balsams and Newfound Mountains. Snow, heavy at times continued into the evening hours. By the time the snow tapered off, accumulations of 2-5 inches were common across the area. However, locally higher amounts occurred, especially in the higher elevations, where up to 10 inches were reported. The heavy wet snow, combined with gusty winds, caused some trees to fall and isolated power outages.

March was ushered in with the biggest and for most of the area the only snowstorm of the 2008-2009 season. A complex and deep surface and upper-level system brought rain to much of the area late 28 February, which changed over to snow in the western HSA. A secondary low pressure area tracked over the Carolinas to the Virginia coast early on March 1, allowing all areas to change over to snow. Snow totals ranged from around a few inches in parts of the the piedmont to over a foot in some mountain locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 to 10 inches with isolated higher amounts across the county caused significant winter travel difficulties. Jefferson reported a storm total of 11 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective vortex moved to the northeast and across the area during the evening. Deep moisture and higher dewpoints were aligned ahead of a pre-frontal trough and surface low pressure in eastern South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey confirmed an EF-2 Tornado with winds up to 115 mph about 4 miles north of Clarkton, North Carolina. The tornado first touched down at a farm along N Mitchell Ford Road. It flipped a silo and damaged a storage building that collapsed onto farm equipment. As the tornado moved north-northeast, just west of N Mitchell Ford Road, it caused minor damage to a house and shed and downed four trees. The tornado emerged from the woods near a house causing minor roof damage, then intensified from an EF-0 Tornado to an EF-2 Tornado as it impacted a house along Twisted Hickory Road. The tornado completely destroyed a cinder-block storage and farm building and sent cinder-blocks into the nearby house, causing considerable damage to the walls and roof. The back of the house lifted off the foundation causing it to buckle. The roof over the porch was completely gone. Debris from this location, which included among many other items, a washing machine, lawn mower and couch was found four hundred yards away from the house. The family took shelter under a stairway and was not harmed. The tornado continued to impact properties on the east side of Twisted Hickory Road and caused minor roof damage to three other homes and knocked down a couple dozen trees. The tornado weakened as it moved north-northeast toward Britt Road, just east of the intersection of Britt Road and Twisted Hickory Road. The tornado lifted just north of Britt Road as it moved toward a swampy area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across the area during the morning. Ahead of the front, moderate instability and favorable shear supported the development of severe weather including tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey concluded an EF-2 tornado with winds up to 115 mph occurred near Clarkton, North Carolina. The tornado initially touched down on Farmers Union Road about 0.3 miles south of Porterville School Road. Significant damage occurred to four homes in this area, including one which was pushed approximately 20 feet off its foundation. A storage trailer weighing more than 8500 pounds was blown approximately 40 feet and flipped upside down as it rotated 180 degrees. A full-size pickup truck was blown 15 feet across a yard and a 12 x 12 feet structure was completely destroyed. Damage in this area was rated as EF-2 with winds up to 115 mph. The width of the damage path in this area was approximately 100 yards. The tornado briefly weakened as it tracked to the northeast. Numerous 12-18 inch diameter treetops were snapped and a few trees up to 18 inches in diameter were uprooted as the tornado approached Chancey Road. Two homes on Chancey Road suffered EF-0 damage primarily to roofs and windows. The tornado continued to the northeast across mainly woods and farmland where spotty EF-0 damage was confined to trees. The tornado strengthened once again as it crossed Baer Mill Road and caused damage to two homes. The front porch of one home was destroyed and the roof covering material on the windward side of the structure was stripped. The wind penetrated the second story of the home and blew out two upstairs windows. Numerous large trees up to two feet in diameter were snapped off or uprooted. One wooden storage shed was completely destroyed. Damage in this area was rated as EF-2 with wind speeds estimated to 115 mph. The width of the damage path was approximately 60 yards at this location. The tornado weakened as it moved northeast from Baer Mill Drive. Spotty minor tree damage was observed between Baer Mill Drive and Susie Baldwin Road. There were injuries or fatalities.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey concluded that an EF-1 tornado with winds up to 105 mph occurred near Longwood, North Carolina. The tornado initially touched down near Etheridge Road NW and State Route 1300. The tornado produced EF-0 damage in this location with two homes suffering minor damage. A small hog pen was destroyed near the two homes and six trees were snapped. Spotty damage was observed to trees as the tornado moved east-northeast across State Route 1300. The tornado peaked at EF-1 with significant damage to over forty trees north of the intersection of Ward Road NW and Etheridge Road NW. It turned to the northeast causing spotty EF-0 damage at the north end of Freedom Star Road. It then crossed Highway 904 and Butler Pond before reaching Butler Circle NW where six trees and a shed were damaged. The tornado lifted before reaching Russtown Road NW. There were no injuries or fatalities.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A super-cell thunderstorm with damaging winds accelerated as it moved across Robeson County. Numerous trees and power lines were down and there was considerable structural damage. A National Weather Service Storm Survey concluded that a wet microburst produced a swath of damaging straight-line winds up to 125 mph. The microburst damage began near the intersection of Wilton Drive and Gem Road. Several trees were uprooted or snapped off and minor to moderate damage was observed to roof shingles and to siding. Significant damage was observed to the east of NC Highway 72. Numerous large trees were snapped off or uprooted along NC Highway 72 and significant structural damage occurred to approximately 8 homes on Sadie Drive. One of these homes was completely destroyed and another lost its entire roof. Several sheds and outbuildings were destroyed in this area. One adult woman suffered broken bones. The damage had a maximum path width of 350 yards and a path length of 2.25 miles. The Robeson County Emergency Manager estimated the damage at $813,000.00.

A thunderstorm complex over Kentucky and Tennessee moved east into the area during the evening of May 8th. The storm environment was such that storms would have rotation due to strong wind shear, and a low level boundary in place. Supercells developed over southeast Kentucky and moved southeast into far southwest Virginia and into northwest North Carolina during the late evening of May 8th. One storm produced an EF-2 tornado in Northern Alleghany County, with another brief EF-1 tornado touching down just south of the EF-2 track.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was also a brief touchdown of an EF-1 tornado near Jarvis Road. This tornado tracked around a  quarter mile and then lifted near Early Road. Numerous trees were snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a stationary front, producing a few reports of large hail over the western Piedmont of North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning ignited a fire at a home in the Bariwood Community, causing significant damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low end EF-2 tornado touched down on Osee Road and was intermittently on the ground approximately 5 miles moving east-southeast, finally lifting between Old Barrett Road and Glade Valley Road. ||A single wide mobile home was destroyed on Osee Road, causing four injuries. Along the track, cement silos were collapsed, 5 other homes were damaged, and several other structures were heavily damaged. Two people received minor injuries when struck by debris after winds blew out windows in their home. Monetary damages are estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced a great deal of lightning over Western North Carolina during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home on Braswell Rd, igniting a fire that damaged a garage apartment.

Broadcast media reported residents said the water level was the highest it had been in at least 15 years. A few rock and mud slides were also reported in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms persisted just inland from the coast during the late evening hours of Jun 12th into the the early morning hours of June 13th 2009. These storms dropped locally very heavy rain and flooding occurred in Havelock.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms producing heavy rain persisted over Havelock for several hours. Doppler radar estimated 3 to 5 inches occurred near Havelock. An elderly woman drove into a flooded loading dock and drowned when trying to get out of her vehicle.

These creeks flooded numerous roads and bridges, including Love Valley Rd, Fox Mountain Rd, Fox Bottom Rd, Burke Farm Rd, and Bacon Rd. Several homes were also flooded in these areas. At least one family required rescue from their home on Morgan Ford Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance and attendant surface cold front combined to produce scattered showers and thunderstorms across the eastern half of central North Carolina. The unseasonably dry low levels of the atmosphere across central North Carolina created a favorable environment for any thunderstorms that developed to produce damaging winds. Many of the thunderstorms that developed became severe and produced damaging winds across the eastern half of central North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several Trees were reported down near Spivey's Corner, North Carolina, near United States Highway 421 and United States Highway 13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two tornadic supercells developed along a stalled warm front that extended across souther piedmont and coastal plain of North Carolina.|The cells produced 4 tornadoes in Johnston, Nash and Wilson counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado touched down initially just to the west of NC Highway 96 in a wooded area. The tornado cross NC Highway 96 and produced significant damage to several homes along and east of NC Highway 96. The homes sustained exterior and roof damage with uprooted or snapped tree damage consistent with EF-1 damage. |The tornado proceed to cross NC Highway 39. Several homes between Antioch Church Road and NC Highway 42 sustained damaged. The tornado continued to track east-northeast, causing roof shingle damage to the Antioch Fire Station and a nearby home. A mobile home near the fire department also sustained shingle and siding damage. The tornado then continued to track northeast across Barnes Lake Road and NC Highway 222, where it caused significant tree damage and some minor roof or shingle damage to surrounding homes or mobile homes. The tornado then |exited the county, crossing over into southwest Nash County.

Convection developed in an area of surface convergence and enhanced low level moisture advection. The thunderstorms intensified during the late afternoon and early evening hours as a mid-level shortwave and jet streak crossed the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey concluded a microburst, with winds up to 90 mph, first impacted the ground just to the west of Oak Grove Church Road and to the north of Highway 242. The microburst moved south across Highway 242 and into a heavily wooded area south of Highway 242. Forty trees were either uprooted or snapped. One tree fell onto a mobile home, injuring one person. The mobile home sustained moderate damage. Another tree crushed a van as it fell. A poorly built mobile home, located on the north side of Highway 242, was destroyed. Minor to moderate damage was also observed to a metal car shop along Highway 242. The path length was 0.7 miles and the path width was 370 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains fell during the early morning hours of June 16th north of a stationary front in the Ararat River basin with 2.19 inches in 2 hours measured at the USGS river gage on that river. The river rose over 9 feet in about 6 hours and flooding occurred along Route 104 north of Mt. Airy, NC.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ararat River reported to be flooding portions of Highway 104 near the state line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front meandered along the North Carolina and Virginia border during the evening of May 3rd. An upper level disturbance also pushed across the region. This created a high shear environment, with enough instability to produce a few thunderstorms. One of these thunderstorms dropped an EF-0 tornado two miles north of Mayodan, NC in northwestern Rockingham County. Damaging winds also occurred with a thunderstorm further east in the northeast portion of the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding developed across the southern and eastern sides of the city after thunderstorms dumped 3 to 5 inches of rain across the area in a couple of hours. Although much of the flooding was due to poor drainage, Briar Creek, Little Sugar Creek, and other small streams flooded. Numerous motorists were trapped and required rescuing due to flood water, with the most serious situations occurring on Independence Bvd near Wendover Rd, Wellesley Ave near Freedom Park, and on East Bvd at Maryland Ave. Some residents had to be evacuated from an apartment complex on Monroe Rd due to flooding along Briar Creek.
