EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical air mass was in place, with a weak front north across Virginia. Southeast flow into the mountains and the frontal boundary set the stage for heavy rain producing showers and thunderstorms. Heavy rains across southern Wilkes County dumped close to 4 inches in a short amount of time. Flash flooding occurred across the Moravian, Hunting and Warrior creek basins...inundating roads and homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moravian Creek flooded and closed Germantown Road near Moravian Falls, and School Street in Wilkesboro. Twenty-seven people were evacuated from their homes along Moravian Creek, near Germantown Road, and six mobile homes were destroyed. Wilkesboro Community College received damage from the flooding of Moravian Creek. The grounds were heavily damaged, as was several buildings. The flooded creek eventually led to the collapse of a 150 foot long, 20 foot wide section of U.S. Highway 421 East exit ramp lane in Wilkesboro, which occurred during the early morning of May 27th. Monetary damages include this road collapse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical moisture remaining in place, along with a weak and slow moving frontal, kept the region in high risk of flash flooding, due to the recent heavy rains. Showers developed in bands, and moved across western Wilkes County during the early morning of May 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stony Fork flooded a portion of Casper-Hawkins Road in Hendrix.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced a few areas of flash flooding for a third straight day over western North Carolina. A couple reports of large hail were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water on Sandymush Creek washed out a private bridge and eroded the soil beneath a section of Sandymush Creek Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving into the region helped trigger showers and thunderstorms across the Blue Ridge and foothills of North Carolina. The storms were slow movers and heavy rain producers. One storm brought flash flooding to the southern portions of Wilkes County during the early evening of the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding closed a 2 mile stretch along Highway 268 near the W. Kerr Scott Reservoir. A mudslide occurred which caused some trees to fall over the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical moisture remaining in place, along with a weak and slow moving frontal, kept the region in high risk of flash flooding, due to the recent heavy rains. Showers developed in bands, and moved across western Wilkes County during the early morning of May 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Prong of Lewis Fork flooded a portion of Daniel Drive, 2 miles southeast of Maple Springs.

Bands of heavy snow moved across Central North Carolina blanketing the region with 2 to 8 inches of snow. Areas in the piedmont experienced the most snow fall with the Triad reporting up to 8 inches. The heavy snow was the result of a surface low pressure system tracking east out of the Ohio River Valley combining with cold air from the north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between three to five inches of snow fell countywide. Several automobile accidents were reported the mornings following the storm due to the re-freezing of the melting snow overnight.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning ignited a fire at a business on W Airline Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed in the afternoon and evening along a surface trough across central North Carolina, in a warm and moist atmosphere. A mid level shear axis crossed the area during the evening helping to intensive the thunderstorms. Some of the thunderstorms during the evening became severe and produced several reports of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power-lines were reported down on North Carolina Highway 56. Numerous power outages were reported across eastern portions of Franklin County as well. One tree was blown onto a house on Edward Best Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving complex of thunderstorms produced flash flooding in metro Charlotte.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding of quite a few roads developed across the north side of the city, mainly due to poor drainage. Most of the flooding was concentrated along Freedom Blvd, where several intersections were under 2-3 feet of water. Flooding extended northeast along portions of Statesville Rd and N Tryon St as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance and attendant surface cold front combined to produce scattered showers and thunderstorms across the eastern half of central North Carolina. The unseasonably dry low levels of the atmosphere across central North Carolina created a favorable environment for any thunderstorms that developed to produce damaging winds. Many of the thunderstorms that developed became severe and produced damaging winds across the eastern half of central North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down near I-95 and North Carolina Highway 87.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms developed along and east of Interstate 77 over the western North Carolina Piedmont during the late evening hours. The slow moving storms produced severe urban flooding along with damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were flooded in Concord, mainly due to poor drainage on the north side of town. Several swift water rescues were required. Water entered a home on Spring St NW and a business on Branchview Dr at highway 73. Urban flooding also developed in Kannapolis, along Jackson St, where additional rescues were required.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms developed along and east of Interstate 77 over the western North Carolina Piedmont during the late evening hours. The slow moving storms produced severe urban flooding along with damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small creek flooded a portion of Bethel School Rd in Midland, and another creek flooded a portion of Stough Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms developed along and east of Interstate 77 over the western North Carolina Piedmont during the late evening hours. The slow moving storms produced severe urban flooding along with damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although heavy rain ended, water levels remained high from the city limits of Concord southwest to Stough Rd for several hours during the early morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable air mass ahead of cold front, along with an strong upper disturbance, contributed to the development of organized thunderstorms on August 5th. A line of thunderstorms developed along the Blue Ridge, and pushed east into the Piedmont of North Carolina during the afternoon and evening. The storms mainly produced damaging winds, resulting in downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on E Manor Ridge Road.

The storms mainly produced damaging winds, resulting in downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed in the Hamptonville area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 20, 2009 the first major winter storm to strike Central North Carolina since 2004 brought 3 to 7 inches of snow to most of the area. A strong upper level disturbace dropped quickly southeast into the mid-Atlantic follwed by a surface of low pressure which developed south of Wilmington. The combination of cold air and moisture surging in from the coast allowed the rain to transition to snow between midnight and 3 am. Snow lasted until afternoon in many locations and did not taper off in the Interstate 95 corridor until 3 pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 5 to 7 inches of snow fell across the county over a 12 hour period. Roads were quickly covered with snow resulting in several traffic accidents and the closing of local schools and businesses. Over 700 automobile accidents were reported in the county along with a few house fires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of heavy snow moved across Central North Carolina blanketing the region with 2 to 8 inches of snow. Areas in the piedmont experienced the most snow fall with the Triad reporting up to 8 inches. The heavy snow was the result of a surface low pressure system tracking east out of the Ohio River Valley combining with cold air from the north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between three to four inches of snow fell countywide. A few automobile accidents were reported the mornings following the storm due to the re-freezing of the melting snow overnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection that originated over the foothills and mountain drifted east and into the Northwest Piedmont of central North Carolina. With a warm and moist air mass in place one storm became severe and produced penny sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny sized hail was reported along South Elm Eugene Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance in combination with a moist and unstable atmosphere resulted in scattered strong to severe thunderstorms across the area during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree branches down near Simpson just east of Greenville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary line of thunderstorms developed just inland of the coast along the sea breeze during the afternoon of August 12, 2009. Torrential rain occurred for several hours centered near Havelock. Rainfall amounts of 6 to 10 inches were reported in a three to four hour period leading to significant flooding in the Havelock area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flooding began in the Havelock area around 145 PM EST as a stationary line of thunderstroms dropped torrential rain for an extended period of time. Rainfall amounts in the Havelock and Cherry Point areas ranged from 6 to 10 in inches and this all occurred mainly between 12 pm EST to 4 pm EST. This rain caused widespread flooding across the city of Havelock with many roads closed with over 2 feet of water on them. Several homes and businesses also had some minor flooding with less than one foot of water reported in the lowest floor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms with heavy rains developed over Yadkin County and southeast Rockingham County on June 10th. These storms developed in advance of an area of low pressure aloft tracking out of the Ohio Valley. The Rockingham storm produced winds gusting up to 60 mph and damage in several locations in Reidsville. In addition, 1 to 2.5 inches of rain in a short period in and around Reidsville, caused some flooding along small streams including Little Troublesome Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds brought down a large tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front rapidly crossed the region during the late evening of September 28th 2009. A line of showers and scattered thunderstorms formed ahead of the front and one storm produced large hail in Martin County.

A strong cold front and powerful upper level trough swept across central North Carolina late in the afternoon into the early evening hours on September 28, 2009. A band of showers and thunderstorms formed as the cold front moved east of the mountains and into central North Carolina. Some of these storms became severe and produced damaging winds and hail to the size of nickels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were reported down on Brooks Lake Road. Approximately 12,000 customers were without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A third round of heavy rain in two days over the southern North Carolina Mountains caused the French Broad River to go into flood west of Hendersonville. The prolonged heavy rain also caused many streams to flood, closing several roads across the southern mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding developed near the headwaters of the French Broad River which covered Main St in Rosman with about a foot of water. Water also entered an apartment building. Also, the Davidson River flooded near Brevard, flooding a portion of Davidson River Rd and Wilson Rd. Turkey Creek flooded Deavor Rd in Pisgah Forest. A total of 35 roads in Henderson and Transylvania Counties were blocked by flood water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A third round of heavy rain in two days over the southern North Carolina Mountains caused the French Broad River to go into flood west of Hendersonville. The prolonged heavy rain also caused many streams to flood, closing several roads across the southern mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A third round of heavy rain in two days over the southern North Carolina Mountains caused the French Broad River to go into flood west of Hendersonville. The prolonged heavy rain also caused many streams to flood, closing several roads across the southern mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding developed in several spots along the Little Tennessee River after an extended period of heavy rainfall. Several roads were closed, including Arthur Drake Rd and Riverside Rd. At least one motorist required rescue after driving over a flooded road. Water entered and damaged several homes along the river. In addition, water from Cartoogechaye Creek surrounded several homes on Patton Creekside Dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary front across the Tennessee valley region continued to aid in the development of very heavy rainfall that contributed to flash flooding that evolved into a large areal flood event across a small portion of southwest North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in areal flooding in the Murphy area, with all streams in vicinity out of their banks and several inches of water was reported to be over and flowing across low lying areas along highway 19.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy rain fell between the 18th and 22nd across the southern mountains of North Carolina. Over the five day period, over 17 inches of rain fall in a few locations, with many areas reporting 10 inches of more. The most significant flooding started early  in the morning on the 20th when three nearly stationary areas thunderstorms produced very heavy rain along the eastern escarpment of the Blue Ridge. Widespread flooding of streams and some large rivers developed over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although flash flooding ended across the area around sunrise, persistent moderate to heavy rainfall caused high water conditions to persist in central Henderson County through the morning hours. Flooding also expanded to areas near Mills River, as Butler Bridge Rd was closed about 8 miles northwest of Hendersonville. In addition to the flooding, saturated soil resulted in quite a few fallen trees and isolated small landslides.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level disturbance combined with an arctic cold front resulted in wind gust of 45 to 55 mph across central North Carolina. Numerous trees were blown down across the area resulting in power outages and minor propery damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts between 45 and 55 mph resulted in numerous trees being blown down along resutling in power outages. One man was killed near Denton on Highway 109 when a tree fell onto his vehicle. Another tree fell on a house causing minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants from Hurricane Ida which made landfall along the Florida panhandle combined with a powerful upper level storm system along the Carolina coast. The result was a powerful low pressure system which developed near Cape Fear and tracked to Cape Hatteras. Between 3 to 6 inches of rain fell across central North Carolina over a 3 day period. Gusty winds to 35 to 45 mph combined with saturated soil resulted in power outages and some property damage from falling trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 40 to 45 mph along with heavy rain resulted in numerous trees falling and knocking down power lines. Trees fell on several homes and three vehicles were destroyed by falling trees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three trees were blown down around Fairview.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down across the western half of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Organized line segments and supercells produced widespread thunderstorm wind damage across central North Carolina as a strong upper shortwave trough and associated 50 to 60 knot mid-level jet streak spread into the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down and snapped along a one mile stretch along Highway 501 at Red Mountain Road to the intersection of Moores Mill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable air mass ahead of cold front, along with an strong upper disturbance, contributed to the development of organized thunderstorms on August 5th. A line of thunderstorms developed along the Blue Ridge, and pushed east into the Piedmont of North Carolina during the afternoon and evening. The storms mainly produced damaging winds, resulting in downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on NC Route 268.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening mesoscale convective complex entered the North Carolina mountains from eastern Tennessee. The storms produced a little wind damage over the lower French Broad Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Bill produced long period swells that caused strong rip currents at Wrightsville Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long period swells from Hurricane Bill produced high surf and strong rip currents at Wrightsville Beach and surrounding communities.  Lifeguards at Wrightsville Beach reported nearly 50 rip current and high surf rescues.  One man was slightly injured and was transported to the hospital for precautionary reasons.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms developed over the Charlotte metro area during the afternoon hours, resulting flooding of urban areas and small streams. An additional area of flash flooding developed later that night over the southern foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding along Brier Creek, covering portions of Brier Creek Rd. Several residents were trapped by the rising water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm with torrential rains caused Kraut Creek to overflow its banks and flood the parking area of the Boone Mall, in Boone, NC, August 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kraut Creek flooded its banks, causing the closure of the Boone Mall entrance and parking lot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain produced another round of isolated flash flooding to portions of northwest North Carolina during the evening of the 20th into early morning on the 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mill Creek flooded its banks, resulting in the road closure of West Mill Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain produced another round of isolated flash flooding to portions of northwest North Carolina during the evening of the 20th into early morning on the 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stoney Creek flooded its banks, closing Hiatt Road. Four people had to be rescued from the high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain produced another round of isolated flash flooding to portions of northwest North Carolina during the evening of the 20th into early morning on the 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kraut Creek overflowed its banks, causing the entrance and parking lot to the Boone Mall to be closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain produced another round of isolated flash flooding to portions of northwest North Carolina during the evening of the 20th into early morning on the 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding caused Brintle Circle to be closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low topped tornadic supercells developed along a stationary front located across the coastal plain region of North Carolina. The storms intensified as a shortwave trough moved northeast across the area, producing EF-1 damage in Cumberland County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five houses on OBJ Road sustained minor damage ranging from shingle damage, damaged garage doors, to blown out windows.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm fired up across southeastern Caswell County, producing nickel sized hail.

Slow moving thunderstorms developed over the Charlotte metro area during the afternoon hours, resulting flooding of urban areas and small streams. An additional area of flash flooding developed later that night over the southern foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding developed across portions of the Charlotte metro area after as much as 4 inches of rain fell over the city in just a few hours. Most of the problems were within the Little Sugar Creek basin, especially in the Wakefield Dr area, where roads were flooded, and some apartment units were evacuated. Water ended the crawl spaces of two homes in this area. The other main problem area was in the Parkwood Rd area northeast of Charlotte, where roads were flooded. Flooding of numerous intersections was reported due to poor drainage, including at Tyvola Rd and I-77, where two vehicles were reported floating through high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms over the North Carolina mountains and foothills produced torrential rains, which led to some flash flooding during the evening of August 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of rain produced flash flooding in West Jefferson, resulting in a utility pole being washed out of the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable air mass ahead of a cold front helped with the formation of thunderstorms. Some of the storms had torrential rains which caused flash flooding over portions of the North Carolina mountains during the afternoon and evening of August 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Little Phoenix Creek flooded a portion of Jake Blackburn Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable air mass ahead of a cold front helped with the formation of thunderstorms. Some of the storms had torrential rains which caused flash flooding over portions of the North Carolina mountains during the afternoon and evening of August 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Boone Creek at the Boone Mall flooded its banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable air mass ahead of a cold front helped with the formation of thunderstorms. Some of the storms had torrential rains which caused flash flooding over portions of the North Carolina mountains during the afternoon and evening of August 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Little Horse Creek and the South Fork of Little Horse Creek flooded portions of Little Horse Creek Road, and Joe Hampton Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms over the North Carolina mountains and foothills produced torrential rains, which led to some flash flooding during the evening of August 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains brought Spice Bottom Creek out of its banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms over the North Carolina mountains and foothills produced torrential rains, which led to some flash flooding during the evening of August 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains caused a tributary of Lousy Creek to come out of its banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm affected portions of Surry County during the evening of August 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down in White Plains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm affected portions of Surry County during the evening of August 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down in Ararat.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm affected portions of Surry County during the evening of August 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One male firefighter was part of a water shuttle operation at a house fire in an area with no hydrants. As the firefighter worked near a pond he was struck. He didn't suffer any long term effects from the strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm affected portions of Surry County during the evening of August 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An Ararat female volunteer firefighter  was helping fight a house fire when a bolt of lightning struck her helmet Thursday night. She was transported to a nearby hospital but had only minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped trigger strong to severe thunderstorms across portions of northwest North Carolina during the afternoon of August 11th. One thunderstorm produced damaging winds in Surry County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Shoals Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm affected portions of Surry County during the evening of August 20th.
Tropical moisture produced from four to six inches of rain in New Hanover and Pender Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain washed out a driveway in Porters Neck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical moisture produced from four to six inches of rain in New Hanover and Pender Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was reported at the intersection of Hwy 17 and Sloop Point Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped trigger strong to severe thunderstorms across portions of northwest North Carolina during the afternoon of August 11th. One thunderstorm produced damaging winds in Surry County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Armstrong Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five houses on OBJ Road sustained minor damage ranging from shingle damage, damaged garage doors, to blown out windows.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and into the early evening, as a shortwave trough aloft interacted with a moist and unstable atmosphere. Hail to the size of quarters and isolated wind damage were reported with these severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized hail was reported by a trained spotter in the Jefferson Park area of Goldsboro. In addition the spotter estimated the maximum wind gust to be around 45 to 50 miles per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large complex of storms moved eastward across Kentucky and Tennessee and into northwest North Carolina on the evening of June 16th. These storms brought damaging winds to pockets of the northwest North Carolina Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was blown down on Georges Gap Road near Sugar Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe storms developed over western North Carolina ahead of a cold front. There was also an isolated instance of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hidden Valley Road was flooded and closed near Mountain Ridge Church Rd due to flooding along White Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Airmass thunderstorms produced a few areas of large hail and damaging winds over the North Carolina Mountains during the late afternoon and evening hours. There was also an isolated instance of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to quarter size hail was reported on Sugar Hollow Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of thunderstorms produced a few areas of wind damage over the North Carolina foothills during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down along Vein Mountain Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong daytime heating featuring afternoon temperatures in the mid to upper 90s resulted in moderate instability across central North Carolina. Isolated convection|developed along a pre-frontal surface trough.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were blown down near Moores Mill and Rassie Crabtree Roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm produced large hail over the western Piedmont of North Carolina during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was reported at the intersection of Double Shoals Rd and Polktown Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms in advance of a cold front produced damaging winds and large hail across portions of northeast North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large trees were downed along Highway 258.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along a surface trough during the early afternoon. These thunderstorms intensified as an upper level jet strengthened across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees up to 10 inches in diameter were down on and around a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning started a couple house fires over western North Carolina during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a large home on Pimlico Ln, igniting a fire that caused enough damage to render the home uninhabitable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Ida interacted with a strong upper level disturbance to produce a deep, slow moving, low pressure system off the Carolina coast November 11th through the 13th. This system produced widespread heavy rain, gusty winds, and coastal flooding across portions of eastern North Carolina. By far, the most signifcant impact was coastal flooding over the northern Outer Banks where over five million dollars in damage was reported. The large waves from the storm continued to batter the Outer Banks for several days after the storm system moved away.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain, with widespread amounts over six inches, flooded numerous secondary roads off Highway 24. Several roads were impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense surface heating along with a surface trough across eastern portions of central North Carolina produced scattered showers and thunderstorms across the coastal plain of North Carolina. Some of these storms became severe and produced hail to the size of nickel and damaging winds. These storms also produced very heavy rain, and several reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized hail was reported at the intersection of North Carolina Highway 42 and Amelia Church Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary surface frontal boundary was draped near the North Carolina-South Carolina border during the morning. A self-sustaining cluster of super-cellular thunderstorms, with a history of severe hail and damaging winds, moved into the area from the west and then off the coast before noon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of golf balls was reported along Red Lighthouse Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe thunderstorms developed just ahead of a weak cold front across eastern North Carolina during the evening of June 13th 2009.

A strong upper level disturbance and attendant surface cold front combined to produce scattered showers and thunderstorms across the eastern half of central North Carolina. The unseasonably dry low levels of the atmosphere across central North Carolina created a favorable environment for any thunderstorms that developed to produce damaging winds. Many of the thunderstorms that developed became severe and produced damaging winds across the eastern half of central North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A town home fire was reported on Canvas Drive in Raleigh, North Carolina, which was caused by an apparent lightning strike that that ran in on the natural gas line. Damage to the town home was unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Organized line segments and supercells produced widespread thunderstorm wind damage across central North Carolina as a strong upper shortwave trough and associated 50 to 60 knot mid-level jet streak spread into the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was blown down on Reeses Store Road at Highway 125.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong warm and moist air advection along the leading nose of a southerly low-level jet|and atop a stationary warm front in place across central North Carolina resulted in|heavy rainfall of 3 to 4 inches. The heaviest rainfall occurred over the Raleigh area |causing mainly urban and small stream flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported in low-lying areas across town near Glendale Drive and Forest Hills Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through western North Carolina during the evening hours. The front was preceded by several severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were blown down around the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of storms developed across central North Carolina downstream of a shortwave trough located over eastern Tennessee. The severe storms produced widespread wind damage with only one report of severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported several trees snapped off at the base 2 miles south of intersection of Interstate 40 and Highway 87. Trees were at least 6 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of storms developed across central North Carolina downstream of a shortwave trough located over eastern Tennessee. The severe storms produced widespread wind damage with only one report of severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fourteen inch diameter tree fell on a house at Chandler Court off of Becketts Ridge Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated thunderstorms produced large hail over the Piedmont and foothills of western North Carolina.

A piedmont surface trough and approaching upper level shortwave produced numerous showers and thunderstorms in a warm moist unstable atmosphere across central North Carolina. Many of these thunderstorms became severe and produced heavy rain, damaging winds, and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported on Wilburn Road, near United States Highway 401.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front and powerful upper level trough swept across central North Carolina late in the afternoon into the early evening hours on September 28, 2009. A band of showers and thunderstorms formed as the cold front moved east of the mountains and into central North Carolina. Some of these storms became severe and produced damaging winds and hail to the size of nickels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was reported down across the intersection of Mt. View Church Road and Caraway Mountain Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread moderate to heavy rain caused mainly nuisance flooding over parts of western North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Urban and poor drainage flooding of highway 70 near Hildebran and Main St in Glen Alpine was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front and seasbreeze front, in combination with a moist and unstable atmosphere, provided the focus for widespread strong to severe thunderstorms across eastern North Carolina during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail reported in Farmville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front triggered thunderstorms over the North Carolina Mountains starting in the early afternoon hours. The storms organized into a slow moving mesoscale convective system as they moved across the foothills and western piedmont. Quite a bit of wind damage, and some large hail, was produced.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down over the western half of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several bands of strong to severe thunderstorms developed across central North Carolina as a a shortwave trough moved across the region. The severe threat quickly |transformed from a hail threat early in the event to a damaging wind episode as shortwave mid-level kinematics increased over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning resulted in several house fires from Albemarle to New London. The entire town of Albermarle was without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm complex tracked along a boundary during the afternoon and evening hours. Most of the storm reports were for thunderstorm wind damage, but a few hail occurrences were also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement personnel reported one tree downed by thunderstorm winds in Murphy along with power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Initial storms developed along a surface trough axis across central and eastern portions of North Carolina. Additional thunderstorms then developed later in the afternoon and evening as a weak upper level shortwave trough moved east across the area.  There was widespread thunderstorm wind damage with the most significant damage concentrated |in the Triad. Flash flooding became a threat during the evening in the northeast coastal plain with several streets closed in Edgecombe and Halifax Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power-lines down near Aventon, some trees were down across area roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several clusters of thunderstorms produced areas of severe weather over parts of western North Carolina during the afternoon and evening hours. Some flash flooding was also observed over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down and a storage building was blown over on Keisler Dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving line of thunderstorms produced wind damage as it crossed the mountains and foothills of North Carolina.
Scattered severe storms affected the mountains and foothills of North Carolina, as well as the Piedmont east of Charlotte, during the early evening hours.
Very unstable air developed well inland from the coast during the afternoon of June 15th 2009. Scattered thunderstorms developed and a few produced large hail and strong winds.

A strong upper trough and attendant surface cold front moved through the coastal plain region of North Carolina during the morning hours. The storms produced penny size hail and isolated damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective vortex moved to the northeast and across the area during the evening. Deep moisture and higher dewpoints were aligned ahead of a pre-frontal trough and surface low pressure in eastern South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey confirmed an EF-0 Tornado touched down about 6 miles north-northwest of White Lake, North Carolina. The tornado briefly touched down near the intersection of Johnsontown Road and Susie Sandhill Road. The tornado moved north-northeast across both roads near the intersection and caused damage to approximately ten trees. Some of the trees were snapped while others were uprooted. No other damage was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated thunderstorms produced large hail over the Piedmont and foothills of western North Carolina.
Afternoon airmass thunderstorms produced large hail over the North Carolina Mountains and western Foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Part of the roof of a carport was also damaged on highway 90 east of Collettsville. A tree was also blown down 4 miles northwest of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Initial storms developed along a surface trough axis across central and eastern portions of North Carolina. Additional thunderstorms then developed later in the afternoon and evening as a weak upper level shortwave trough moved east across the area.  There was widespread thunderstorm wind damage with the most significant damage concentrated |in the Triad. Flash flooding became a threat during the evening in the northeast coastal plain with several streets closed in Edgecombe and Halifax Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water from flash flooding reported across Morning Star Church Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the area late in the afternoon into the early evening on May 29th. Thunderstorms developed along the Blue Ridge and moved eastward into the Piedmont. Some of these storms became severe producing penny size hail.
Heavy rain caused a few problems in western North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of heavy rain and a damaged culvert caused the dirt beneath a section of Brevard Rd to wash away, collapsing the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms in advance of a cold front produced damaging winds across portions of northeast North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were downed.
